title: Estimating Spatiotemporal Pumping Amounts Using Multiple Signal Decomposition Methods

research.question: Can regional groundwater pumping be reconstructed from dense groundwater-level observations when individual pumping-well records are unavailable?
research.system: Four aquifers of the Choshui River alluvial fan in Taiwan observed hourly at 197 monitoring stations from 2008 through 2019.
research.inputData: Twelve years of hourly groundwater level, hourly rainfall, and spatial storage-coefficient fields obtained from pumping tests or calibrated MODFLOW results.
research.method: Use EEMD and Hilbert spectral analysis to organize groundwater fluctuations by period, use rotated EOF patterns to remove spatially broad recharge modes, extract localized 1-7 day pumping modes, convert head change to flow, and interpolate regional pumping.
research.benchmark: The inferred annual pumping range is compared with previous survey, electricity, hydrograph, and physics-based estimates; temporal patterns are compared with cropping demand and spatial drawdown with observed land subsidence.
research.uncertainty: Conditional storage-coefficient fields produce the reported annual range. Additional structural uncertainties arise from subjective recharge-mode identification, mode mixing, monitoring resolution, kriging in nonstationary geology, high-permeability insensitivity, and storage-coefficient error.
research.conclusion: The data-driven reconstruction estimates 1.71-2.05 billion m^3/year, within the previously reported 0.9-2.2 billion m^3/year range, with high pumping concentrated in midwestern Yunlin and seasonal patterns consistent with agricultural demand.

narrative.throughline: Unmix the regional groundwater signal by frequency and spatial footprint until the localized pumping component remains, then convert that component back into a map of extracted water.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: A monitoring-well hydrograph contains overlapping pumping, recharge, tide, trend, and noise signals.
narrative.stakes: More than 200000 mostly undeclared wells prevent direct regional pumping inventories needed for water balance and subsidence management.
narrative.resolution: Dense hourly heads supply enough temporal and spatial structure to isolate pumping-like modes and reconstruct an uncertainty-bounded regional extraction history.

background.premise: Undeclared pumping wells make direct groundwater-use information inadequate for regional management.
background.visibleEvidence: A regional map fills with many unlabeled pumping wells while the official pumping ledger remains sparse.
background.whyNow: Missing pumping information impairs groundwater-use assessment and water-balance estimation.
background.boundary: The paper estimates aggregate spatiotemporal pumping rather than identifying individual illegal wells.
background.sourceExcerpt: situation, with over 120,000 illegal wells pumping groundwater
background.visualObject: Choshui fan map with unregistered well symbols and a missing-data ledger
background.motionPurpose: Make the observation gap spatially concrete.
background.stepDetail: Populate the fan with well symbols, then fade most entries from the inventory table.
background.transitionBridge: Use groundwater levels as the indirect record shared by all pumping activity.

background.premise: GWL fluctuations form a time series that mixes forcings with different temporal frequencies and spatial footprints.
background.visibleEvidence: One hydrograph decomposes into broad recharge, coastal tide, localized pumping, trend, and noise layers.
background.whyNow: Pumping must be separated from natural variability before head change can be converted to extraction.
background.boundary: The separation is data driven and depends on the paper's temporal-period and spatial-pattern interpretation.
background.sourceExcerpt: Viewing GWL fluctuations as a time series, the study categorizes
background.visualObject: stacked signal components beneath an observed hydrograph
background.motionPurpose: Frame pumping estimation as a signal attribution problem.
background.stepDetail: Split the observed line into components with distinct period and spatial extent.
background.transitionBridge: Exploit the dense regional monitoring archive to perform that separation.

background.premise: The Choshui GWL data were recorded hourly across four aquifers for twelve years.
background.visibleEvidence: 197 monitoring points pulse hourly across a four-layer fan from 2008 to 2019.
background.whyNow: High space-time resolution is the prerequisite for resolving localized short-period pumping signals.
background.boundary: This premise establishes the observed archive coverage only; monitoring-resolution limitations are reserved for the final claim boundary.
background.sourceExcerpt: The GWL data used in this study were recorded hourly and col-
background.visualObject: four-aquifer monitoring network and twelve-year timeline
background.motionPurpose: Show the observational support behind the data-driven inference.
background.stepDetail: Pulse stations in hourly cadence and extend the timeline through all twelve years.
background.transitionBridge: Decompose each station's nonstationary record without prescribing fixed basis frequencies.

methods.input: Hourly groundwater-level series at each monitoring station.
methods.output: Seven to eight identified IMF components plus a residual trend per selected series.
methods.validity: EEMD adds white noise with amplitude 0.002 over 1500 ensembles to reduce mode mixing in nonstationary GWL records.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.2, Equations 1-2; PDF pages 6 and 8, Section 3.1 and Figure 7
methods.sourceExcerpt: which enhances IMF identification by adding white noise to the
methods.visualObject: one hydrograph unfolding into ordered IMF bands
methods.motionPurpose: Separate nonstationary oscillations without imposing fixed sinusoidal bases.
methods.stepDetail: Add small noise realizations, sift each ensemble, and average corresponding IMFs.
methods.transitionBridge: Assign a representative frequency to every IMF.

methods.input: Instantaneous phase of every IMF from Hilbert spectral analysis.
methods.output: One average frequency or period for each station-specific IMF.
methods.validity: The method assumes the long-term average response frequency to a particular forcing is more stable than its instantaneous frequency.
methods.sourceExcerpt: stable. The HHT framework employs Hilbert Spectral Analysis (HSA) to
methods.visualObject: IMF oscillation with an instantaneous-frequency trace collapsing to a mean marker
methods.motionPurpose: Create a comparable period coordinate across stations with different IMF counts.
methods.stepDetail: Trace phase speed through time and collapse it to the reported average frequency.
methods.transitionBridge: Group IMFs with similar representative periods.

methods.input: Mean IMF frequencies from all monitoring locations.
methods.output: Mean-shift classes of comparable IMF periods.
methods.validity: Mean Shift Clustering is nonparametric and density based; the study uses bandwidth 0.105 and does not require a preset number of clusters.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.2, Equation 4; PDF page 8, Figure 8
methods.sourceExcerpt: Mean Shift Clustering (MSC) method was emplyed, a non-parametric, density-based clustering algorithm.
methods.visualObject: period markers migrating toward density peaks
methods.motionPurpose: Align comparable oscillatory scales across the monitoring network.
methods.stepDetail: Shift each period marker toward its neighborhood mean until cluster centers stabilize.
methods.transitionBridge: Resolve the spatial patterns within each common frequency class.

methods.input: Spatially distributed IMFs belonging to the same frequency class.
methods.output: Orthogonal spatial EOF patterns and temporal expansion coefficients.
methods.validity: Singular value decomposition separates a spatiotemporal field into orthogonal spatial functions and corresponding temporal projections.
methods.sourceExcerpt: (SVD), decomposes a spatiotemporal random field into orthogonal
methods.visualObject: frequency-class matrix separating into orthogonal spatial maps and temporal coefficients
methods.motionPurpose: Expose the orthogonal space-time factors within one frequency class.
methods.stepDetail: Separate the class matrix into spatial EOFs and temporal projections by SVD.
methods.transitionBridge: Apply the rotated EOF representation in M05.

methods.input: Orthogonal EOF spatial functions and temporal projections from one IMF class.
methods.output: Rotated spatial EOF patterns and corresponding expansion coefficients.
methods.validity: The paper applies rotated EOF analysis to IMFs already grouped in the same frequency class.
methods.sourceExcerpt: the rotated EOF method was applied to IMFs in the same class.
methods.visualObject: orthogonal EOF axes turning into rotated spatial patterns
methods.motionPurpose: Orient the common-frequency modes for physical interpretation.
methods.stepDetail: Apply the rotated EOF method to one common-frequency class.
methods.transitionBridge: Identify broad recharge-linked modes in M06.

methods.input: Rotated EOF spatial patterns and expansion coefficients compared with rainfall-derived signals.
methods.output: EOF modes identified as recharge-associated signals.
methods.validity: Recharge is assigned to modes with broad spatial patterns and rainfall-consistent timing; the paper acknowledges subjective judgment and treats deeper confined aquifers differently because recharge is less pronounced.
methods.locator: PDF pages 4 and 8-9, Section 2.2; Section 3.2, Figure 9
methods.sourceExcerpt: frequencies to identify those representing large spatial patterns linked
methods.visualObject: broad EOF map synchronized with a rainfall-related temporal coefficient
methods.motionPurpose: Assign a physical recharge meaning to selected data-driven modes.
methods.stepDetail: Identify the modes whose spatial extent and temporal coefficients support recharge attribution.
methods.transitionBridge: Combine the identified recharge modes in M07.

methods.input: Recharge-associated groundwater variations from the identified frequency classes.
methods.validity: Equation 7 defines cumulative recharge variation across the recharge-associated frequency classes.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The cumulative groundwater variation due to recharge across dif-
methods.visualObject: recharge-frequency layers accumulating into one broad groundwater field
methods.motionPurpose: Reconstruct the total recharge-associated variation from its identified classes.
methods.stepDetail: Combine the recharge-associated frequency fields into h_R.
methods.transitionBridge: Subtract the reconstructed recharge field in M08.

methods.input: Observed groundwater variation h(x,t) and cumulative recharge variation h_R(x,t).
methods.output: Recharge-removed groundwater variation containing pumping-related signals a nonstationary trend and random noise.
methods.validity: Equation 8 subtracts cumulative recharge variation after treating recharge and pumping as the primary external factors affecting groundwater.
methods.locator: PDF pages 4-5, Section 2.2, Equation 8; PDF page 10, Figure 10
methods.sourceExcerpt: The time series of original GWL data, GWL with recharge signal removed, and IMF components derived from GWL with recharge signal removed at Citong(1) station.
methods.visualObject: total GWL field minus a broad recharge layer
methods.motionPurpose: Make the residual signal physically interpretable before the second decomposition.
methods.stepDetail: Fade the broad recharge field from each station trace and preserve the remaining fluctuations.
methods.transitionBridge: Decompose the recharge-removed residual in M09.

methods.output: A second set of IMFs from the recharge-removed groundwater signal.
methods.validity: The study explicitly applies HHT again after recharge removal before selecting pumping-frequency components.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.2, paragraph before Equation 9; PDF page 10, Figure 10
methods.sourceExcerpt: In this study, HHT was employed again to decompose
methods.visualObject: recharge-removed hydrograph unfolding into a second IMF stack
methods.motionPurpose: Expose residual time scales before assigning a pumping window.
methods.stepDetail: Decompose the recharge-removed signal with HHT.
methods.transitionBridge: Extract the one-to-seven-day components in M10.

methods.input: IMFs decomposed from the recharge-removed groundwater signal.
methods.output: Pumping-associated IMFs with periods from one to seven days.
methods.validity: The stated one-to-seven-day range reflects frequencies typical of pumping activity in the study area; signals outside this signature may be missed.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.2, paragraph before Equation 9; PDF page 10, Figures 10-11
methods.sourceExcerpt: in the study area (e.g., one to seven days). These IMFs represent the
methods.visualObject: second IMF stack retaining only one-to-seven-day components
methods.motionPurpose: Isolate the temporal window attributed to local agricultural pumping.
methods.stepDetail: Extract IMFs within the stated one-to-seven-day pumping window.
methods.transitionBridge: Combine the retained pumping modes in M11.

methods.input: Pumping-associated IMFs within the selected period window.
methods.validity: Equation 9 defines the pumping-associated groundwater variation as the sum of the selected signals.
methods.visualObject: selected pumping IMFs accumulating into one pumping-associated hydrograph
methods.motionPurpose: Reconstruct the pumping-associated head variation from the retained modes.
methods.stepDetail: Combine the selected pumping-associated IMF signals into h_P.
methods.transitionBridge: Calculate pumping-associated head-change rate in M12.

methods.input: Pumping-associated groundwater variation h_P(x,t) at two time points.
methods.output: Station-based pumping-associated head-change rates over the selected intervals.
methods.validity: Equation 10 uses the pumping-associated drawdown difference over the elapsed interval before storage conversion.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The drawdown difference between two time points
methods.visualObject: pumping-associated hydrograph segmented into rectangular head-change pulses
methods.motionPurpose: Isolate the temporal head-change quantity before any spatial or storage conversion.
methods.stepDetail: Measure each pumping-associated head difference and divide it by the selected interval.
methods.transitionBridge: Interpolate only the station head-change rates across the fan in M13.

methods.output: Continuous pumping-associated head-change-rate field across the alluvial fan.
methods.validity: Ordinary kriging is a practical choice under limited geological data but may be inadequate for nonstationary geological properties.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.2; PDF page 11, Figure 12
methods.sourceExcerpt: ordinary kriging method was first used to estimate the rate of pumping-
methods.visualObject: station head-change rates spreading into a continuous fan map
methods.motionPurpose: Construct the regional head-change field without converting it to pumping amount.
methods.stepDetail: Expand kriging influence from each station until a continuous head-change-rate field covers the fan.
methods.transitionBridge: Apply storage and affected area to the interpolated head field in M14.

methods.input: Kriged pumping-associated head-change rates with affected area and conditional storage-coefficient fields.
methods.output: Spatiotemporal pumping-rate fields carrying storage-coefficient uncertainty.
methods.validity: Equation 10 multiplies head-change rate by affected area and elastic storage; conditional random fields carry interpolated storage uncertainty into pumping estimates.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.2, Equation 10; PDF page 8, Section 3, paragraph describing Figure 12
methods.sourceExcerpt: which was accounted for by applying a conditional random field
methods.visualObject: conditional storage maps multiplying the kriged head-change field
methods.motionPurpose: Perform the storage conversion only after the head field has been constructed.
methods.stepDetail: Multiply the head-change-rate field by affected area and each conditional storage field.
methods.transitionBridge: Aggregate the converted pumping fields into a regional time series in M15.
methods.minimalText: Q = S A Delta h / Delta t

methods.input: Conditional spatial pumping-rate fields across the Choshui aquifer.
methods.output: Monthly pumping-rate time series for the entire aquifer.
methods.validity: Figure 12b reports monthly pumping rates aggregated over the Choshui aquifer from 2008 to 2019.
methods.sourceExcerpt: pumping rates of the entire aquifer from 2008 to 2019
methods.visualObject: mapped pumping cells accumulating into a monthly aquifer-wide series
methods.motionPurpose: Create the regional time series without assigning annual totals to this operation.
methods.stepDetail: Aggregate each conditional pumping field across the aquifer for every monthly interval.
methods.transitionBridge: Calculate the reported ensemble interval in M16.

methods.input: Annual average pumping totals from the conditional storage-field realizations.
methods.output: An annual pumping interval of 1.71-2.05 billion m^3/year.
methods.validity: The reported range incorporates storage-coefficient uncertainty but excludes other structural uncertainties in signal attribution and monitoring coverage.
methods.locator: PDF page 8, Section 3, paragraph describing Figure 12a
methods.sourceExcerpt: an estimated annual average pumping amount of
methods.visualObject: annual aquifer totals settling into the reported uncertainty band
methods.motionPurpose: Express the conditional annual totals as the reported numerical interval.
methods.stepDetail: Calculate the lower and upper annual pumping totals from the conditional realizations.
methods.transitionBridge: Carry the regional pumping result into the temporal demand comparison in M17.

methods.input: Monthly regional pumping-rate series and regional agricultural groundwater-demand pattern.
methods.output: Similar temporal patterns between estimated pumping rates and regional agricultural groundwater demand.
methods.validity: The conclusion reports qualitative temporal similarity with regional agricultural groundwater demand rather than a numerical goodness-of-fit measure.
methods.sourceExcerpt: distribution of estimated pumping rates presents similar patterns with
methods.visualObject: monthly regional pumping curve aligned with the agricultural groundwater-demand pattern
methods.motionPurpose: Make the paper's reported temporal comparison visible without implying a quantified fit.
methods.stepDetail: Align the estimated monthly pumping curve with the regional agricultural groundwater-demand pattern and mark their reported similarity.
methods.transitionBridge: Carry the temporal comparison into the spatial drawdown-subsidence comparison in M18.

methods.input: Estimated pumping-induced drawdown map and observed land-subsidence pattern for the study period.
methods.output: Spatial agreement between estimated pumping-induced drawdown and the most serious land-subsidence areas.
methods.validity: The conclusion reports spatial consistency for the study period while the discussion notes that gravel-dominated proximal-fan areas can show high drawdown with relatively mild subsidence.
methods.locator: PDF page 12, Section 5, Conclusions; PDF pages 9 and 11, Section 4 and Figure 13
methods.sourceExcerpt: estimated pumping-induced drawdown is consistent with the areas
methods.visualObject: estimated pumping-induced drawdown map paired with the observed accumulated-subsidence map
methods.motionPurpose: Show the paper's spatial comparison while preserving its geological qualification.
methods.stepDetail: Pair the drawdown and accumulated-subsidence maps and highlight the reported coincident serious-subsidence areas.
methods.transitionBridge: Return both source-grounded comparisons with the annual interval to the Choshui aquifer conclusion.

symbols.meaning: groundwater-level variation at location x and time t
symbols.transition: The observed field decomposes into IMF bands.

symbols.meaning: intrinsic mode function j at a monitoring location
symbols.transition: Each band receives an average Hilbert frequency.

symbols.meaning: average instantaneous frequency of IMF j
symbols.transition: Frequency markers migrate into mean-shift clusters.

symbols.meaning: cumulative groundwater-level variation attributed to recharge
symbols.transition: The broad field is subtracted from total variation.

symbols.meaning: summed groundwater-level variation attributed to pumping
symbols.transition: The signal converts to a volumetric rate.

symbols.meaning: pumping flowrate derived from pumping-associated head change
symbols.transition: The kriged head-change field converts to a regional pumping-rate field.

return.visualObject: the four-aquifer Choshui fan with the bounded annual total beside the temporal demand comparison and drawdown-subsidence map pair
return.claim: Dense hourly groundwater levels support a regional pumping reconstruction of 1.71-2.05 billion m^3/year with the paper's reported temporal agricultural-demand similarity and spatial land-subsidence consistency.
return.claimBoundary: The range includes storage-coefficient field uncertainty but not all attribution error. Sparse or coarse monitoring can miss pumping, very permeable zones can yield underestimated rates, recharge selection is partly subjective, and ordinary kriging may not represent nonstationary geology.
return.locator: PDF pages 8-12, Figures 12-13, Discussion and Conclusions
return.sourceExcerpt: groundwater level changes can be insensitive to pumping activities, it is
return.minimalText: Regional pumping from dense head signals

visual.primaryObject: four-aquifer Choshui River alluvial fan monitoring network
visual.accentObject: IMF frequency bands resolving into a regional pumping map
visual.measuredQuantity: hourly groundwater-level variation converted to pumping rate and annual volume
visual.geometryNotes: Use the fan-shaped Taiwan study area, 197 station locations, four aquifer layers, coastal tide influence, broad recharge footprints, localized pumping footprints, and the reported midwestern Yunlin pumping concentration.
